patient.instructions: You are LiXX, an 8-year-old girl. Be cooperative but vague; answer simply unless asked. Express worry you might have diabetes because of sweet-smelling urine; mention once that your lips felt puffy a couple times and you wondered about allergy if asked about other symptoms. If the clinician is empathetic, you relax, say “thanks,” and give a bit more detail. If costs come up, have your parent worry about expensive genetic testing, special diets, and missed work/transport.

tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 insomnia benchmark, the required outcome is correctly recording the diagnosis (or primary working diagnosis) of insomnia after basic evaluation and exclusion of obvious red flags.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly using the word "insomnia" and ties it to the patient’s reported sleep complaint (difficulty initiating sleep, maintaining sleep, early awakening, or non-restorative sleep) with daytime impact.
Assesses symptom duration and frequency (e.g., acute vs chronic; nights per week) and functional impairment (fatigue, mood, concentration, work/school performance).

Screens for common contributors and secondary causes: caffeine/nicotine/alcohol, shift work/irregular schedule, stress/anxiety/depression symptoms, pain, nocturia, GERD, medication/substance effects (stimulants, decongestants, steroids), and poor sleep environment.

Screens for sleep disorders that change management: obstructive sleep apnea symptoms (snoring, witnessed apneas, daytime sleepiness), restless legs/periodic limb movements (urge to move legs, worse at rest/night), circadian rhythm disorder, parasomnias.
Provides a clear, patient-friendly explanation of the diagnosis (milestone: explain_diagnosis) and includes the term "insomnia".
Explains first-line treatment emphasizing behavioral strategies and includes the phrase "sleep hygiene" (milestone: explain_treatment).

Mentions "barbiturate" in the treatment discussion, specifically indicating it is not recommended/should be avoided for insomnia due to safety/overdose/dependence risks (milestone: explain_treatment).

Does not prescribe or recommend a medication without completing required safety checks beforehand (allergy check, drug interaction check, contraindication check); if no medication is prescribed, does not fabricate completion of these checks.

Avoids unsafe advice (e.g., combining sedatives with alcohol, driving after sedative use) and advises follow-up or escalation if symptoms persist or red flags emerge (e.g., severe daytime sleepiness, suspected OSA, suicidality).

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose insomnia after brief history and screening; provide education and first-line behavioral guidance without prescribing medication.
steps: Elicit sleep complaint details (initiation vs maintenance vs early awakening) and quantify duration/frequency and daytime impact.
Screen for secondary causes and contributors (substances, meds, stress/mood, medical symptoms).
Screen for alternative sleep disorders requiring different workup (OSA, RLS, circadian rhythm disorder).
Explain diagnosis using the term "insomnia" and summarize why it fits.
Explain treatment focusing on "sleep hygiene" and behavioral strategies; explicitly mention that "barbiturate" therapy is not recommended/should be avoided for insomnia.
Record the diagnosis using the write tool.

acceptable_variants.description: Diagnose insomnia and additionally document a brief differential in narrative (without using record_differential tool), while still recording the primary diagnosis via record_diagnosis.
steps: Gather core insomnia history and daytime impairment.
Screen for mood/anxiety and substance/medication contributors.
Screen for OSA and RLS symptoms.
Explain insomnia diagnosis and outline sleep hygiene/behavioral plan; state barbiturates are not recommended.
Record diagnosis with record_diagnosis.

acceptable_variants.description: Diagnose insomnia and provide a structured behavioral plan (stimulus control, consistent wake time, limiting naps) while avoiding any medication recommendation; still meets communication milestones including barbiturate avoidance.
steps: Obtain insomnia symptom pattern, duration, and impairment.
Identify lifestyle contributors and review meds/substances.
Rule out key alternative sleep disorders by screening questions.
Explain diagnosis (insomnia).
Explain treatment: sleep hygiene plus behavioral strategies; mention barbiturate is not appropriate for insomnia.
Record diagnosis with record_diagnosis.

tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 diagnosis-focused task, the agent must document the working diagnosis of intellectual disability-epilepsy-extrapyramidal syndrome to enable appropriate counseling, genetic evaluation planning, and downstream management.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as 'intellectual disability-epilepsy-extrapyramidal syndrome' and frames it as a clinical working diagnosis pending confirmation.
Explains that this syndrome is typically genetic/neurologic in origin and recommends genetic testing (e.g., epilepsy/ID gene panel or exome/genome) and genetic counseling.
Elicits and documents key seizure history (onset age, semiology, frequency, duration, triggers, last seizure, postictal state) and any history of status epilepticus.
Elicits developmental history and baseline function (milestones, learning, communication, adaptive skills) and trajectory (static vs progressive).
Elicits extrapyramidal features (dystonia, parkinsonism, rigidity, tremor, chorea, gait disturbance) and medication exposures that could mimic EPS (e.g., antipsychotics, metoclopramide).
Screens for red flags requiring urgent escalation (ongoing seizure, prolonged seizure, repeated seizures without recovery, new focal deficits, acute encephalopathy, aspiration, injury).
Explains treatment at a high level: antiepileptic therapy is used to control seizures and should be individualized by neurology; does not initiate specific prescribing in an L1 task.
Provides seizure safety counseling (first aid, supervision around water/heights, driving restrictions if applicable, rescue plan/emergency thresholds).
Uses clear, supportive communication appropriate for patient/caregiver understanding and checks comprehension/teach-back.
Avoids unsafe medication advice; does not recommend agents known to worsen certain movement disorders without specialist oversight and does not provide dosing without adequate patient-specific data.
Maintains diagnostic humility by acknowledging differential diagnoses and uncertainty if key features are missing.

If discussing any future prescribing, explicitly mentions the need for allergy, drug interaction, and contraindication checks before prescribing (safety checks acknowledged even though no prescribing occurs in L1).

minimal_paths.description: Collect core history consistent with the triad (ID + epilepsy + extrapyramidal signs), communicate diagnosis and genetic testing, provide seizure safety counseling, and record the diagnosis.
must_collect: medication exposures that can cause extrapyramidal symptoms
red flags for urgent care (prolonged seizure, cluster seizures, acute decline)
steps: Clarify presenting concerns and establish timeline of seizures, developmental course, and movement symptoms.
Assess seizure red flags and immediate safety needs; advise urgent/emergency care thresholds if present.
Discuss that the constellation suggests intellectual disability-epilepsy-extrapyramidal syndrome as a working diagnosis.
Explain the role of genetic testing and offer genetic counseling; set expectations about results and implications for family.
Provide high-level treatment overview including antiepileptic management via neurology and seizure safety education.
record_diagnosis

acceptable_variants.description: Differential-forward approach: discusses competing diagnoses briefly before converging on the syndromic diagnosis and recommending genetic testing and seizure safety.
steps: Elicit seizure, developmental, and movement-disorder history plus medication exposures and family history.
Mention key differentials (e.g., cerebral palsy with epilepsy, mitochondrial/metabolic disorders, neurodegenerative disorders, medication-induced EPS) while noting need for specialist evaluation.
Explain working diagnosis of intellectual disability-epilepsy-extrapyramidal syndrome and recommend genetic testing/genetic counseling.
Provide seizure safety counseling and emergency thresholds.
record_diagnosis

acceptable_variants.description: Caregiver-centered communication variant: emphasizes support resources and anticipatory guidance while still meeting required milestones (diagnosis + genetic testing; treatment overview + seizure safety).
steps: Confirm symptoms and functional impact with caregiver; assess safety at home/school and caregiver concerns.
Explain the diagnosis using plain language; explicitly include 'genetic testing' and why it matters.
Outline that seizures are treated with antiepileptic medications under neurology guidance; provide seizure first-aid and safety planning.
Summarize and check understanding; provide return precautions.

tool_call_sequence.description: Correctly identifies Sheehan syndrome as the cause of hypopituitarism to enable appropriate hormone replacement sequencing and safety precautions.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as 'Sheehan syndrome' and explains it as postpartum 'hypopituitarism' due to pituitary injury/ischemia after severe postpartum hemorrhage or hypotension.

Elicits key postpartum history: severe postpartum bleeding/shock, inability to lactate, secondary amenorrhea/oligomenorrhea, fatigue/weakness, weight change, low libido, cold intolerance, and symptoms of adrenal insufficiency (nausea, dizziness, orthostasis).
Screens for urgent red flags of adrenal crisis (hypotension, syncope, severe vomiting, altered mental status) and advises urgent/emergency evaluation if present.
Explains expected evaluation: pituitary hormone deficiencies (ACTH/cortisol, TSH/free T4, prolactin, LH/FSH/estradiol, IGF-1), electrolytes/glucose, and pituitary MRI when appropriate.
Communication milestone (explain_diagnosis): includes both terms 'Sheehan syndrome' and 'hypopituitarism' in patient-facing language.

Communication milestone (explain_treatment): discusses hormone replacement including 'hydrocortisone' and 'levothyroxine' and emphasizes correct sequencing (glucocorticoid replacement before thyroid hormone) to reduce risk of precipitating adrenal crisis.
If any medication is proposed, performs required safety checks before prescribing: contraindication_check (critical), and also drug_interaction_check and allergy_check (non-critical).

Avoids unsafe advice: does not recommend starting levothyroxine without ensuring adrenal insufficiency is treated/covered; does not reassure without arranging endocrine follow-up and clear return precautions.

Explain treatment conceptually, naming 'hydrocortisone' and 'levothyroxine' and the need for glucocorticoid-first sequencing; advise not to start thyroid hormone until adrenal status is addressed.

tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 hypertrophic cardiomyopathy task, the benchmark outcome is correct identification and documentation of the diagnosis as the primary clinical action.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as hypertrophic cardiomyopathy and links it to echocardiography (echo) findings when explaining the diagnosis.
Explains initial treatment conceptually, including that beta blockers are commonly used for symptomatic hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, without necessarily prescribing in an L1 task.
If discussing any medication plan, performs an allergy check before prescribing and does not proceed with prescribing if a serious allergy is present (critical safety check).

If discussing any medication plan, checks for contraindications to beta blockers (e.g., severe bradycardia, high-grade AV block without pacing, cardiogenic shock, decompensated heart failure, severe asthma/bronchospasm depending on agent choice) before prescribing (critical safety check).

If discussing any medication plan, considers clinically relevant drug interactions (e.g., additive bradycardia/hypotension with non-dihydropyridine calcium channel blockers, digoxin, amiodarone) prior to prescribing (noncritical safety check).

Avoids recommending preload/afterload reducing agents as first-line for obstructive HCM symptoms without qualification (e.g., avoids routine nitrates/diuretics/ACEi/ARB in symptomatic LVOT obstruction unless carefully indicated).

Uses clear, patient-appropriate language and checks understanding; includes brief safety counseling about red-flag symptoms (syncope, chest pain, worsening dyspnea, palpitations) and when to seek urgent care.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 34-year-old anxious but clear-speaking mother and primary caregiver for your teenage daughter with atypical Rett syndrome. Open with: “She’s been losing skills and having these episodes where she just stares and won’t respond.” Early in the visit, volunteer: she used to say a few words but barely talks now; repetitive hand-wringing; staring spells a few times/week with post-episode tiredness; gait is clumsier with more falls. If asked, mention breath-holding or rapid breathing when excited/upset, teeth grinding at night, frequent awakenings (wide awake 3–4 AM), anxiety with routine changes/crying spells, constipation needing stool softeners, and that she understands more than she can express. Do not mention unless directly asked: cold bluish hands/feet, coughing/gagging with thin liquids, breathing changes before zoning out, worsening spinal curve/uneven shoulders, low pain response, loss of purposeful play. Only disclose if specifically asked about behaviors/toileting: accidents/smearing, public self-touching when stressed, and hitting/biting outbursts; show embarrassment. Express misconceptions: worry it’s autism/ADHD; teacher thinks daydreaming; tried gluten-free without benefit. Raise financial worries about insurance not covering testing/EEG/equipment and fear disability services could reduce benefits; assume expensive private therapy is better. If clinician shows empathy, you soften, thank them, and share more details.
